section.title: The Fall of Saul
section.sectionRationale: The Chronicler briefly repeats Saul's genealogy one more time, almost verbatim, immediately before narrating his death, then moves with startling speed and theological economy through the battle, Saul's suicide, the Philistines' desecration of his body, and Jabesh-gilead's brave rescue of his remains. Unlike the extended narrative in 1 Samuel 28 and 31, the Chronicler compresses the entire account and adds his own explicit verdict in 10:13-14: Saul died for his unfaithfulness. This is the hinge on which the whole book turns from genealogy to the story of David.
section.thesis: The Chronicler retells Saul's genealogy and death with striking brevity, then supplies his own inspired verdict, unfaithfulness, failure to keep God's word, consulting a medium, and not seeking the LORD, so that Saul's tragic end becomes not merely sad history but a theological lesson: the throne now passes to David, the king who will seek the LORD, setting up everything that follows in this book.
section.frameNote: Seven beats: Saul's descendants named once more; his genealogy repeated in full; Israel's rout and the death of Saul's sons; Saul's own death by his sword; the Philistines' desecration of his body; the courage of Jabesh-gilead; and the Chronicler's own explicit verdict on why Saul died.

verses.text: Jeiel the father of Gibeon lived in Gibeon. His wife's name was Maacah.
Abdon was his firstborn son, then Zur, Kish, Baal, Ner, Nadab,
Gedor, Ahio, Zechariah, and Mikloth.
Mikloth was the father of Shimeam. They too lived alongside their relatives in Jerusalem.

units.label: Saul's genealogy repeated
units.note: the same line as chapter 8, once more, right before his death

verses.text: Ner was the father of Kish, Kish was the father of Saul, and Saul was the father of Jonathan, Malchishua, Abinadab, and Esh-baal.
The son of Jonathan: Merib-baal, who was the father of Micah.
The sons of Micah: Pithon, Melech, Tahrea, and Ahaz.
Ahaz was the father of Jarah; Jarah was the father of Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri; and Zimri was the father of Moza.
Moza was the father of Binea. Rephaiah was his son, Elasah his son, and Azel his son.
And Azel had six sons, and these were their names: Azrikam, Bocheru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. These were the sons of Azel.

units.label: Israel's rout and the death of Saul's sons

verses.text: Now the Philistines fought against Israel, and the men of Israel fled before them, and many fell slain on Mount Gilboa.
The Philistines followed hard after Saul and his sons, and they killed Saul's sons Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchishua.

units.label: Saul's death by his own sword

verses.text: When the battle intensified against Saul, the archers overtook him and wounded him.

Then Saul said to his armor-bearer, "Draw your sword and run me through with it, or these uncircumcised men will come and torture me!" But his armor-bearer was terrified and refused to do it. So Saul took his own sword and fell on it.
When his armor-bearer saw that Saul was dead, he too fell on his own sword and died.
So Saul died together with his three sons and all his house.

units.label: The Philistines desecrate Saul's body
units.note: the head of Saul in the temple of Dagon

verses.text: When all the Israelites in the valley saw that the army had fled and that Saul and his sons had died, they abandoned their cities and ran away. So the Philistines came and occupied their cities.
The next day, when the Philistines came to strip the dead, they found Saul and his sons fallen on Mount Gilboa.
They stripped Saul, cut off his head, took his armor, and sent messengers throughout the land of the Philistines to proclaim the news in the temple of their idols and among their people.
They put his armor in the temple of their gods and hung his head in the temple of Dagon.
When all the people of Jabesh-gilead heard about everything the Philistines had done to Saul,
all their men of valor set out and retrieved the bodies of Saul and his sons and brought them to Jabesh. And they buried their bones under the oak in Jabesh and fasted seven days.
So Saul died for his {{maal|unfaithfulness}} to the LORD, because he did not keep the word of the LORD and even consulted a {{ov|medium}} for guidance,
and he failed to {{darash|inquire}} of the LORD. So the LORD put him to death and turned the kingdom over to David son of Jesse.

maal.gloss: Unfaithfulness, treachery, a breach of covenant trust against the LORD. The Chronicler uses this same word to explain both Judah's exile, 1 Chronicles 9:1, and now Saul's death, tying the nation's judgment and its first king's judgment to the identical root cause: a failure of covenant loyalty to God.

ov.gloss: A medium, a spirit of the dead, or one who consults such a spirit; the practice is explicitly forbidden in Leviticus 19:31 and 20:6. Saul's recourse to a medium at Endor, 1 Samuel 28, becomes for the Chronicler the concrete evidence of his deeper unfaithfulness, seeking guidance from the forbidden rather than from the LORD.

darash.gloss: To seek, inquire of, or search out, often used of seeking the LORD in prayer, worship, or through His appointed means. Saul's fatal failure was that he did not darash the LORD; by contrast, seeking the LORD, darash, becomes a repeated, defining mark of David and the faithful kings who follow him throughout Chronicles.

basic.context: This passage briefly names Gibeon's founding family, then repeats Saul's own genealogy, almost word for word from chapter 8, immediately before telling of his death. The battle account itself is compressed into a few verses: Israel routed on Mount Gilboa, Saul's sons killed, Saul himself wounded by archers and then falling on his own sword rather than be captured, his armor-bearer following him in death. The Philistines desecrate his body, displaying his armor and head in their temples, until the brave men of Jabesh-gilead recover his remains under cover of night and give him a proper, if hurried, burial. Then, in just two verses, the Chronicler delivers his own inspired verdict: Saul died because of his unfaithfulness, his failure to keep God's word, his consulting of a medium, and his failure to seek the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, told with unusual compression compared to 1 Samuel's fuller account, because the Chronicler's interest is theological rather than merely biographical. He is not writing a full life of Saul; he assumes his audience already knows that story from Samuel. What he adds, and what only he adds, is the explicit divine verdict of verses 13 and 14, turning a battlefield tragedy into a lesson about what true kingship requires: faithfulness to God and a heart that seeks Him.
basic.originalLanguages: The Chronicler's verdict rests on three key words: Saul died for his {{maal|unfaithfulness}}, having consulted an {{ov|medium}}, and having failed to {{darash|seek}} the LORD. That last word, darash, to seek or inquire of God, becomes a defining mark of the faithful kings who follow Saul throughout Chronicles, so that Saul stands, from the very start, as the photographic negative of what a godly king should be.
basic.typology: Saul, the king who trusted a medium instead of seeking the LORD and whose apparent final humiliation, his head displayed in the temple of Dagon, becomes total defeat, stands as a dark warning against every merely human king. He anticipates, by contrast, the perfectly faithful King, Jesus, who never failed to seek and obey His Father, and whose apparent defeat on the cross became, unlike Saul's, the very means of ultimate and everlasting victory.
basic.christ: Saul is judged for failing to seek the LORD; Jesus is the King who always sought, and always did, His Father's will, faithful to Him who appointed Him, Hebrews 3:2. Where Saul's death handed the kingdom to David by God's decree, this whole book is building toward the greater truth that the kingdom is finally and forever handed to great David's greater Son, whose throne, unlike Saul's, will never be given to another.
basic.god: God is shown as the sovereign judge of kings: it is the LORD who put Saul to death and turned the kingdom over to David, verse 14. His judgment is neither arbitrary nor merely political; it flows directly from Saul's own persistent unfaithfulness. Yet even in this severe scene of judgment, God's larger redemptive purpose, to give His people a faithful king, is already moving forward.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 28:3-25 gives the fuller account of Saul consulting the medium at Endor. 1 Samuel 31 narrates the same battle and death in more detail. Hosea 13:11 reflects on Saul as the king God gave in anger. Hebrews 3:1-6 contrasts a merely faithful servant with the Son who is faithful over God's house as a son.
basic.oneStory: Saul's fall, told here with sober brevity and a clear divine verdict, closes the door on Israel's first experiment in human kingship and opens the door to David, whose reign, worship, and covenant will occupy the rest of this book. The pattern set here, judgment on unfaithful kingship and the LORD's sovereign choice of His anointed, points forward to the final and unshakable kingship of Jesus Christ, who never failed to seek His Father and whose throne will never be given to another.

context.literary: The Chronicler frames Saul's death with his genealogy repeated twice, once in chapter 8 and again immediately here in 9:35-44, so that the reader meets Saul's family in full detail right before watching that family collapse. This repetition is deliberate bracketing, turning what could be read as a mere battle report into a family tragedy with covenantal weight, capped by the Chronicler's own explicit theological verdict in verses 13-14, material without a direct parallel in 1 Samuel.
context.historical: The battle of Mount Gilboa, around 1010 BC, ended Israel's first royal dynasty in national disaster: Israelite territory lost to the Philistines, the king and his heirs dead, and the royal corpse subjected to public humiliation, a common ancient Near Eastern practice meant to proclaim the total defeat of a rival king and his god. Jabesh-gilead's rescue of the bodies recalls Saul's earlier deliverance of that very city, 1 Samuel 11, a debt of loyalty repaid at real risk.
context.canonical: This chapter functions as the hinge of 1 Chronicles, closing nine chapters of genealogy and opening the long narrative of David that occupies the rest of the book. By compressing Saul's story to its theological essentials and stating the divine verdict so plainly, the Chronicler prepares his post-exilic readers, who already knew the monarchy had ultimately fallen too, to see both judgment and hope: unfaithful kings fall, but God's purposes for a faithful king continue.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To compress Saul's death into its sharpest theological form, supplying an explicit divine verdict, unfaithfulness, medium-consulting, and failure to seek the LORD, so that the transition to David's kingship would be understood as God's own righteous judgment and gracious provision, not mere political accident.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific historical judgment on a specific king; it does not prescribe that every failure results in sudden death. What it does teach for every reader is the abiding principle behind the judgment: faithfulness to God and seeking Him are non-negotiable marks of a life, and especially a leadership, that pleases Him, while unfaithfulness and forbidden shortcuts to guidance invite ruin.

debates.question: Why does the Chronicler compress Saul's story so drastically compared to 1 Samuel's extensive narrative of his rise, reign, and conflict with David?

views.label: To move quickly to David, the Chronicler's true subject
views.case: 1 Chronicles is fundamentally a book about David and the covenant of worship God makes with him; the Chronicler assumes his audience already knows Saul's fuller story from Samuel and needs only the theological bottom line before introducing David properly.

views.label: To avoid dwelling on Israel's first failed experiment with human kingship
views.case: By skipping Saul's early promise and focusing only on his catastrophic end, framed by his own genealogy, the Chronicler keeps his post-exilic audience's attention fixed on hope rather than lingering over a discouraging chapter of national history they already knew all too well.

debates.bearing: Both explanations point to the same pastoral aim: the Chronicler wants his readers looking forward to David, and beyond David to God's covenant faithfulness, not backward at Saul's cautionary failure.

debates.question: Does the Chronicler's verdict, that Saul died for consulting a medium and failing to seek the LORD, add new information to or simply summarize 1 Samuel's narrative of his battlefield death?

views.label: The Chronicler supplies the ultimate divine cause behind the visible one
views.case: 1 Samuel narrates the historical, visible sequence of events, wounds, despair, suicide; 1 Chronicles pulls back the curtain to name the spiritual cause, that the LORD put him to death because of his covenant unfaithfulness, showing the battlefield death as the outworking of a prior spiritual verdict.

views.label: The Chronicler distills material already present in Samuel into its sharpest point
views.case: 1 Samuel 28:6-7, 15-19 already records that Saul consulted the medium at Endor because the LORD did not answer him, and that Samuel's ghost pronounced his coming death; the Chronicler is simply summarizing material already in Samuel rather than adding new facts.

debates.bearing: Either way, both books agree completely: Saul's physical death on Gilboa was not a random battlefield tragedy but the culmination of his own persistent unfaithfulness and failure to seek the LORD.

study.originalLanguages: The Chronicler's verdict in verses 13-14 rests on a tight cluster of words: Saul died for his {{maal|unfaithfulness}}, a covenant-breaking treachery against the LORD; he consulted an {{ov|medium}}, a spirit of the dead expressly forbidden in the law; and, most fundamentally, he failed to {{darash|seek}} the LORD. That final verb, darash, will recur again and again across Chronicles as the defining mark of a faithful king, so that Saul's failure to seek is set up, from this very first narrative scene, as the standard every king after him will be measured against.

typology.type: Saul, the king who consulted a medium instead of seeking the LORD
typology.fulfillment: A dark contrast to Christ, the perfectly faithful King who always sought and did His Father's will, Hebrews 3:2.

typology.type: Saul's apparent defeat, his head displayed in the temple of Dagon, becoming total and final humiliation
typology.fulfillment: Reversed in Christ, whose apparent defeat on the cross became, unlike Saul's, the very means of everlasting victory over every false god, Colossians 2:15.

study.christ: Saul is judged because he did not seek the LORD; Jesus is the King who was always faithful to Him who appointed Him, Hebrews 3:2, never once failing to seek and to do His Father's will. Where Saul's unfaithful death handed the kingdom over to David by God's own decree, verse 14, the whole sweep of Chronicles builds toward the greater truth this scene only foreshadows: the kingdom is finally and forever given to great David's greater Son, whose throne, unlike Saul's, will never be given to another and whose apparent defeat at the cross was in fact the world's true and final victory over sin and death.
study.god: God is shown here as the sovereign judge of kings: it is explicitly the LORD who put Saul to death and turned the kingdom over to David, verse 14, not merely the fortunes of war. His judgment is neither arbitrary nor merely political; it flows directly from Saul's own persistent unfaithfulness, his failure to keep God's word, and his search for guidance in forbidden places rather than in the LORD Himself. Yet even in this severe and sobering scene of judgment, God's larger redemptive purpose, to raise up a faithful king after His own heart, is already quietly moving forward.

crossReferences.note: Gives the fuller account of Saul consulting the medium at Endor, the specific unfaithfulness this chapter names in summary.
Narrates the same battle and Saul's death in more extended detail, the parallel account the Chronicler compresses here.
Reflects on Saul as the king God gave in anger and took away in wrath, echoing this chapter's severe verdict.
Contrasts a faithful servant in God's house with the Son who is faithful over God's house as a son, the ultimate answer to Saul's unfaithfulness.

study.oneStory: Saul's fall, told here with sober brevity and capped by the Chronicler's own clear divine verdict, closes the door on Israel's first experiment in human kingship and swings the door open to David, whose reign, worship, and covenant will occupy the rest of this book. The pattern set here, judgment on unfaithful kingship paired with the LORD's sovereign choice of His anointed, points forward across the centuries to the final, unshakable kingship of Jesus Christ, who never once failed to seek His Father, and whose throne, unlike Saul's, will never be given to another.

quiz.q: Why does the Chronicler repeat Saul's genealogy in 9:35-44, so soon after giving it in chapter 8?

options.text: To frame Saul's family in full detail immediately before narrating his death, turning the battle report into a family tragedy with covenantal weight.
options.note: The deliberate repetition brackets the account of Saul's fall with his own household, sharpening its impact.

options.text: Because the Chronicler forgot he had already given this genealogy earlier.
options.note: Unlikely and dismissive; the repetition is a recognized deliberate literary technique in Chronicles, not an oversight.

options.text: To show that Saul's family had grown even larger since chapter 8.
options.note: Misses the point; the genealogy is essentially identical, not expanded, and its purpose is placement, not new content.

options.text: There is no connection between this genealogy and the account of Saul's death.
options.note: False; the placement is deliberate and immediately precedes the death narrative.

quiz.q: According to 1 Chronicles 10:13-14, what was the Chronicler's own explicit verdict on why Saul died?

options.text: His unfaithfulness to the LORD, failing to keep His word, consulting a medium, and failing to seek the LORD.
options.note: Verses 13-14 state this verdict plainly and directly, unique material not found in this exact form in 1 Samuel.

options.text: Simply that the Philistine army was too strong for Israel that day.
options.note: True as a surface, military-level description, but it misses the deeper theological verdict the Chronicler explicitly supplies.

options.text: That Saul was unfairly targeted despite his personal faithfulness to God.
options.note: Directly contrary to the text, which names his unfaithfulness as the reason for his death.

options.text: The text offers no explanation for Saul's death.
options.note: False; verses 13-14 give one of the clearest theological verdicts in the whole book.

quiz.q: What did the Philistines do with Saul's body and armor after his death?

options.text: They stripped him, cut off his head, and displayed his armor and head in the temples of their gods, including the temple of Dagon.
options.note: Verses 9-10 describe this public desecration and proclamation of victory.

options.text: They gave him an honorable burial out of respect for a worthy opponent.
options.note: The opposite of what happened; the text describes desecration and humiliation, not honor.

options.text: They returned his body to Israel immediately without incident.
options.note: Inaccurate; it was the men of Jabesh-gilead who later retrieved the body at great risk, not the Philistines who returned it.

options.text: They left his body completely untouched on the battlefield.
options.note: False; the Philistines actively stripped, decapitated, and displayed his remains.

quiz.q: Why did the men of Jabesh-gilead risk their lives to recover Saul's body?

options.text: In loyal gratitude, recalling Saul's earlier deliverance of their city, they retrieved and honorably buried his remains.
options.note: This echoes Saul's rescue of Jabesh-gilead in 1 Samuel 11, a debt of loyalty repaid here at real risk.

options.text: Because the Philistines had ordered them to remove the bodies.
options.note: Not stated in the text; their action is one of voluntary loyalty, not compliance with an enemy order.

options.text: Because they wanted to claim Saul's throne for their own city.
options.note: No support in the text; their motive is honor and loyalty, not a political power grab.

options.text: The text does not explain why they acted.
options.note: False; the wider biblical context, especially 1 Samuel 11, makes their motive of loyal gratitude clear.

quiz.q: How does Saul's fall set up the rest of 1 Chronicles?

options.text: It closes the account of Israel's first, failed royal experiment and hands the kingdom to David, whose faithful reign and covenant will occupy the rest of the book.
options.note: Verse 14 explicitly states that the LORD turned the kingdom over to David, the pivot point of the entire book.

options.text: It shows that kingship itself was a mistake and would soon be abandoned.
options.note: Overstates the point; the text critiques Saul's unfaithfulness specifically, not the institution of kingship, which continues gloriously through David.

options.text: It has no bearing on anything that follows in Chronicles.
options.note: Understates the point; this chapter is the deliberate hinge into the entire David narrative.

options.text: It shows that David seized the throne by force from Saul's family.
options.note: False; the text says the LORD Himself turned the kingdom over to David, not that David seized it violently from Saul.

questions: Saul's tragedy came from failing to seek the LORD and turning instead to forbidden guidance. Where are you tempted to seek answers or comfort from sources other than God?
The Chronicler gives a blunt, honest verdict on Saul rather than softening his failure. How comfortable are you with honest, unflinching evaluation of your own spiritual life?
Jabesh-gilead risked everything out of loyal gratitude for a kindness done long before. Who in your life deserves that kind of costly, remembered loyalty from you?

Saul's apparent final humiliation, his head displayed by his enemies, looked like total defeat, yet it set the stage for David's rise, and ultimately for Christ. Where might what looks like defeat in your life actually be part of God's larger, faithful plan?

The word darash, to seek the LORD, marks the difference between Saul and the faithful kings who follow him. What would it look like for you to actively seek the LORD this week, rather than merely hoping things work out?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that oak can also be rendered terebinth or great tree, referring to a large, well-known tree in Jabesh where Saul's bones were buried.

Unfaithfulness renders the Hebrew maal, a strong covenant term also used in 1 Chronicles 9:1 to explain Judah's exile, linking Saul's personal downfall to the nation's larger pattern of covenant breach.
Inquire renders the Hebrew darash, to seek or search out; this verb becomes a recurring measure of faithful versus unfaithful kingship throughout the rest of Chronicles.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 9:35-10:14. No em dashes or en dashes were present in this passage's source text; none remain in this file.
